content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Non-structural compensatory reform (leave selection age unchanged)'. Hungary cannot feasibly implement a structural reform eliminating or significantly delaying early selection before 2030 without (a) substantial prior teacher-workforce stabilization in rural and STEM areas, (b) explicit sequencing away from concurrent National Core Curriculum and AI-integration reforms, and (c) multi-party legislative consensus to protect against political reversal—only the first precondition is currently in progress.
opportunities: Achievement gains in the range of Portugal's observed 0.3 SD improvement over roughly 15-18 years are plausible for the comprehensive (non-tracked) majority of Hungarian pupils if curriculum, autonomy, and assessment reforms are implemented with comparable fidelity.

Politically less disruptive than structural reform since it does not touch the statutory definition of secondary education, avoiding the felmenő-rendszer obligations and the direct Fidesz 'totalitarian unified model' framing risk that S3 faces — though this should not be read as evidence the package is politically risk-free, given that targeted funding for disadvantaged schools is independently a fiscal-consolidation target.
strengths: Hungary's teaching workforce is structurally aging and contracting: approximately 50% of full-time primary teachers are age 50 or older, only 7.2% are under 30, and total teacher numbers have declined by over 21,000 since 2003/2004 (164,000 in 2023/2024 vs. 185,000+ in 2003/2004).

Teacher shortages in mathematics and natural sciences are acute; fewer than 3,400 people applied for university pedagogical degrees in 2024, with history–English combinations dominating demand for other subjects.
threats: Targeted TEIP-style funding lines are likely first-line cuts during fiscal consolidation (deficit projected at 6.2% in 2026, debt rising to 76.8% of GDP by 2027), making the central benefit-delivery mechanism budget-vulnerable in a way that significantly undermines the durability of any expected equity gain, even though the scenario avoids the structural-reform reversal risks facing S1-S3.

The scenario's political attractiveness as a 'lower-risk' alternative may itself become a political liability if framed by reform advocates or the European Commission as inadequate given Hungary's documented status as the EU's most socially selective system, risking accusations of policy avoidance rather than genuine reform.
weaknesses: The actual magnitude of net positive equity gain in Hungary from delayed selection (holding school-choice and catchment rules constant) remains unknown; Polish gains may not generalize given Hungary's significantly higher baseline segregation and denser network of small rural schools.

The cost (in transition staffing, retraining, and administrative disruption) of a full or partial phase-out of early selection in Hungary is unquantified; Polish and other OECD countries' transition budgets are not published in comparable form.
